metadata.summary: Core Gemini API setup, model selection, text generation, streaming, and chat conversations for foundational LLM integration tasks.

content.title: Gemini API Basics
content.summary: Core concepts, setup, text generation, streaming, and chat conversations. This methodology covers model selection (2.0 Flash vs. 1.5 Pro vs. Thinking), initialization, basic text generation with configuration, streaming responses, multi-turn chat, safety settings, and error handling with retry logic.

why.p: Gemini 2.0 Flash and Gemini 1.5 models are production-ready with excellent cost-to-capability ratios. Flash is the fastest and cheapest, suitable for most agent pipeline steps; Pro offers 2M token context and better reasoning; Thinking models provide explicit chain-of-thought for complex problems. Understanding these tradeoffs upfront prevents architecture pivots later.

The Gemini SDK is simpler than competing APIs for basic use cases: authentication is a single function call, streaming requires one parameter, and chat sessions automatically maintain history. The main pitfall is misunderstanding safety settings and error modes — this methodology establishes the mental model early.

list.item: Starting a new project that will use Gemini and needs auth, model selection, and generation config established.
Exploring Gemini model capabilities (Flash vs. Pro vs. Thinking) before committing to an architecture.
Building a simple text generation or chat pipeline that does not yet need multimodal or function calling.
The team needs a working reference implementation of streaming, async, and JSON output modes.
The task requires function calling or tool use — see gemini-api-integration for that pattern.
You need file uploads (audio/video/large docs) — use the Files API from gemini-api-integration.
The context window needed exceeds what Flash supports and you have not benchmarked Pro vs. Flash trade-offs yet.
You are integrating with Google Cloud enterprise infra — start with Vertex AI patterns, not AI Studio keys.

section.name: model-overview
section.p: Gemini offers multiple model variants; select based on task complexity, speed, and cost constraints.

rule.statement: For new projects, default to Gemini 2.0 Flash unless explicit requirements force a different choice. Use Gemini 1.5 Pro only for complex reasoning or contexts >500K tokens. Use Gemini 2.0 Flash Thinking for math, logic puzzles, and complex multi-step reasoning.
rule.rationale: Flash is 10x cheaper than Pro and fast enough for most agent pipeline steps. The cost difference justifies the minor quality trade-off for bounded tasks. Pro is necessary only for long-context analysis or open-ended reasoning. Thinking models expose reasoning steps explicitly, beneficial for debugging complex logic but not needed for simple tasks.

example.title: Model selection decision tree
example.note: This pattern minimizes cost while matching model to task. Flash is the right default in ~80% of cases.

section.name: installation-and-setup
section.p: Install the SDK, authenticate, and configure the client.

rule.statement: Install the official google-generativeai SDK via pip. For authentication, use environment variable GOOGLE_API_KEY when working with Google AI Studio (free tier or development). For production, use Google Cloud and Vertex AI with gcloud application-default login (no API key in code).
rule.rationale: Environment variables prevent credentials from being checked into git. For production, Vertex AI with workload identity is the secure path; it avoids API keys entirely.

example.title: Python SDK installation
example.code:
# Install import subprocess subprocess.run(["pip", "install", "google-generativeai"], check=True)
# Or add to requirements.txt # google-generativeai>=0.6.0
example.note: Pin a minimum version to avoid breaking changes in minor releases.

example.title: API authentication (Google AI Studio)
example.code:
import google.generativeai as genai import os
# Option 1: Configure explicitly genai.configure(api_key=os.environ.get("GOOGLE_API_KEY"))
# Option 2: Auto-configure from env # genai.configure() # Reads GOOGLE_API_KEY automatically
example.note: Never hardcode API keys. Use environment variables or 1Password vaults in production.

example.title: Vertex AI authentication (Google Cloud)
example.code:
import vertexai from vertexai.generative_models import GenerativeModel
# Authenticate via gcloud # gcloud auth application-default login
vertexai.init(project="your-project-id", location="us-central1") model = GenerativeModel("gemini-1.5-pro")
example.note: Vertex AI uses IAM roles; no API key needed. Preferred for production and regulated workloads.

section.name: basic-text-generation
section.p: Generate text from a prompt using various configurations.

rule.statement: Always set max_output_tokens in generation_config. Omitting it allows the model to generate arbitrarily long responses, multiplying cost unexpectedly. Choose 1024–8192 depending on task.
rule.rationale: Output token count directly determines cost. Unlimited output has caused budget blowouts in production; it is a common oversight with high consequence.

example.title: Simple text generation
example.code:
import google.generativeai as genai
genai.configure(api_key="YOUR_API_KEY") model = genai.GenerativeModel("gemini-2.0-flash")
response = model.generate_content("Explain quantum computing in simple terms") print(response.text)
example.note: Minimal setup; suitable for one-off requests or prototyping.

example.title: Text generation with config
example.code:
model = genai.GenerativeModel( model_name="gemini-1.5-pro", generation_config={ "temperature": 0.7, # Creativity "top_p": 0.95, # Diversity "top_k": 40, # Vocabulary breadth "max_output_tokens": 2048, # Output length limit "response_mime_type": "text/plain", } )
response = model.generate_content("Write a product description for a smartwatch") print(response.text)
example.note: Full config allows tuning response quality and cost.

example.title: JSON output mode
example.note: Structured output with schema ensures consistent, parseable responses.

section.name: streaming
section.p: Stream responses for real-time output or incremental processing.

example.title: Text streaming
example.note: Streaming renders responses as they arrive; flush=True ensures real-time display.

example.title: Chat streaming
example.note: Streaming works with chat; each send_message can return streamed chunks.

example.title: Async streaming
example.code:
import asyncio import google.generativeai as genai
async def stream_response(): model = genai.GenerativeModel("gemini-2.0-flash")
response = await model.generate_content_async( "Explain machine learning", stream=True )
async for chunk in response: print(chunk.text, end="")
asyncio.run(stream_response())
example.note: Async streaming uses a different event loop; do not mix sync and async calls in the same function.

antipattern.description: Mixing sync model.generate_content() and async model.generate_content_async() in the same script.
antipattern.reason: They use different event loop implementations; this causes RuntimeError: This event loop is already running. Pick one pattern and stick to it.

section.name: chat-conversations
section.p: Build multi-turn conversations where context is maintained automatically.

rule.statement: Use model.start_chat() to initialize a session. Persist the chat object across turns; do not create a new chat for each message. The history is automatically maintained by the model.
rule.rationale: Chat history enables context-aware follow-ups. Creating new chats for each turn loses all prior context, forcing repetition in prompts and wasting tokens.

example.title: Basic chat
example.code:
model = genai.GenerativeModel("gemini-1.5-pro") chat = model.start_chat()
response = chat.send_message("Hello! I'm learning Python.") print(response.text)
response = chat.send_message("What should I learn first?") print(response.text)
# Access history for message in chat.history: print(f"{message.role}: {message.parts[0].text[:100]}...")
example.note: Chat automatically includes prior messages in context for each new message.

example.title: Chat with system instruction
example.code:
model = genai.GenerativeModel( model_name="gemini-1.5-pro", system_instruction="""You are a helpful Python tutor. Always provide code examples. Explain concepts step by step. Use beginner-friendly language.""" )
chat = model.start_chat() response = chat.send_message("What are decorators?") print(response.text)
example.note: System instructions establish consistent behavior for the entire chat session.

antipattern.description: Rebuilding chat history from scratch for each message instead of persisting the chat object.
antipattern.reason: Rebuilding discards prior context, forcing you to re-include prior messages in each new prompt. This wastes tokens and breaks natural conversation flow.

section.name: safety-settings
section.p: Configure safety filters for different content policies.

rule.statement: Always check response.prompt_feedback.block_reason before accessing response.text. If the prompt is blocked, response.candidates will be empty and indexing response.candidates[0] will raise IndexError. Wrap text access in a helper that returns None on block, then log the block reason for audit.
rule.rationale: Safety blocks occur on the prompt, not the output; blocked responses have no candidates. Accessing .text on a blocked response raises ValueError. Logging block reasons helps identify if a class of inputs is systematically blocked.

example.title: Configuring safety thresholds
example.note: Each category has a threshold; BLOCK_MEDIUM_AND_ABOVE is the default and recommended starting point.

example.title: Safe response access
example.note: Checking block_reason prevents IndexError and ValueError.

section.name: error-handling
section.p: Handle common exceptions and implement retry logic.

example.title: Exception handling
example.note: BlockedPromptException and StopCandidateException are common and worth handling separately.

example.title: Exponential backoff for rate limits
example.note: Exponential backoff is standard for transient rate limit errors.

antipattern.description: Assuming ResourceExhausted errors on the free tier are transient and will resolve with retries.
antipattern.reason: Free tier daily quotas are hard limits; once exceeded, no amount of backoff helps. Check when quota resets and wait until then, or upgrade to paid tier.

section.name: pricing-and-cost-optimization
section.p: Understand Gemini pricing tiers and optimize for cost.

rule.statement: Use Gemini 2.0 Flash by default for cost optimization; it is 10-40x cheaper than Pro depending on context size. Enable context caching only when the same large prompt prefix (system prompt + document) appears in >5 requests per session; below that, the overhead is not worth it. Set max_output_tokens explicitly to avoid runaway cost.
rule.rationale: Flash pricing is ~$0.10 per 1M input tokens; Pro is $1.25+. Context caching saves 75% on cached tokens but only amortizes if the same prefix is reused many times. Most sessions are one-off questions; caching helps only in high-volume document analysis scenarios.

example.title: Cost-aware generation
example.note: Flash is the right choice for most tasks; Pro is for long context or complex reasoning.

rule.statement: Use gemini-2.0-flash as the default for agent pipeline steps; only escalate to Pro when context >128K tokens or quality is clearly insufficient for the task.
rule.rationale: Flash is fast and cheap; reserving it for 80% of tasks keeps costs down while maintaining quality for the 20% that need Pro's reasoning.

rule.statement: Do not mix -latest model aliases (e.g., gemini-1.5-pro-latest) in production. Pin specific model versions (e.g., gemini-1.5-pro-002) so version upgrades are intentional and tested.
rule.rationale: -latest aliases silently upgrade the model on Google's schedule; production pipelines should control upgrade timing and test before rolling out.

rule.statement: Persist chat.history across turns; validate the last response before appending to history to prevent inconsistency after blocked responses.
rule.rationale: Chat history is mutable; if you append to it after a blocked response, the history becomes inconsistent and subsequent messages lose context.

rule.statement: Store API keys in environment variables or managed vaults (1Password, Google Cloud Secret Manager); never check them into git. For Vertex AI in production, use workload identity federation instead of service account JSON files.
rule.rationale: Credentials in git are compromised immediately; service account files are difficult to rotate. Workload identity is the secure production pattern.

section.name: agent-integration
section.p: Guidance for using Gemini basics in agentic workflows.

rule.statement: A subagent setting up Gemini basics should configure API key from env, select the model based on task complexity (Flash for speed, Pro for long context, Thinking for math), set generation_config with explicit max_output_tokens and temperature, and wrap the call with safety check + retry logic.
rule.rationale: This workflow ensures credentials are secure, cost is bounded, safety is checked, and transient errors are retried.

example.title: Agentic prompt: basic setup
example.code: # Recommended subagent workflow pattern prompt = """ Initialize a Gemini 2.0 Flash model with temperature=0.3, max_output_tokens=2048. Request JSON output using response_mime_type="application/json" with schema: {schema}. Handle BlockedPromptException and ResourceExhausted with exponential backoff (max 3 retries). """
example.note: This pattern encapsulates the core setup, configuration, and error handling.

example.title: Agentic prompt: chat session
example.code: # Recommended pattern for multi-turn chat prompt = """ Start a Gemini chat session with system_instruction: "{system}". Send the following messages in sequence: {messages}. After each response, log usage_metadata.prompt_token_count and candidates_token_count. Return the final response and total token usage. """
example.note: Logging usage per turn enables cost monitoring and optimization.

checklist.check: Verify API key has proper permissions
Select appropriate Gemini model for task
Add retry logic for rate limits
Test chat sessions with system instructions
